standard_description: Use an apostrophe to form contractions and frequently occurring possessives.
subject: Language

khan_exercises.title: Meet the apostrophe
khan_exercises.description: Apostrophes have many important uses in the English language, and this exercise will give you an introduction to those uses!
topic_path: Punctuation: end-of-sentence punctuation, the comma, and the apostrophe

items.question_content:
**What is the apostrophe doing in this sentence?**

>That is Andrew$\redD{\text{'}}$s binder.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Apostrophes have two major functions: they can *shorten expressions*, standing in for missing letters, and they can *show ownership*.

For example:

* *I'm not home.*
* *Howard's cat is grey.*

In the first example, an apostrophe helps shorten "**I am**" to just "**I'm**".

In the second example, an apostrophe shows that the cat **belongs** to Howard.
**In this sentence, the apostrophe shows the "binder" belongs to "Andrew".**

choices.content: The apostrophe shows the `binder` belongs to `Andrew.`

items.question_content:
**What is the apostrophe doing in this sentence?**

>Adannaya$\redD{\text{'}}$s going to win the race for student body President.


[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:

In the first example, an apostrophe helps shorten "**I am**" to just "**I'm**".

In the second example, an apostrophe shows that the cat **belongs** to Howard.
**In this sentence, the apostrophe is shortening "Adannaya is".**

choices.content: The apostrophe shows the `race` belongs to `Adannaya.`

khan_exercises.title: Introduction to contractions
khan_exercises.description: Apostrophes help shorten words by creating contractions. Learn how to spot them!
topic_path: Punctuation: end-of-sentence punctuation, the comma, and the apostrophe

items.question_content:
**Pick the answer that correctly uses a contraction in this sentence.** 

>People always say you \_\_\_\_\_\_ swim immediately after you eat.


[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Contractions are used to shorten expressions by standing in for missing letters.

For example:

* I will → I'll
* Do not → don't
The appropriate contraction for "can not" is **can't**.

khan_exercises.title: Apostrophes and plurals
khan_exercises.description: Apostrophes are generally not used to form English plurals, but there are a few exceptions.
topic_path: Punctuation: end-of-sentence punctuation, the comma, and the apostrophe

items.question_content:
**Choose the option that correctly pluralizes the missing word in this sentence.** 

>All Carl ever wants to talk about is \_\_\_\_\_\_.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
Typically, apostrophes are **not** used to form plurals.

Correct | Incorrect 
- | - 
${\text{three oranges}}$ | $\redD{\text{three orange's}}$
${\text{flat screen TVs}}$ | $\redD{\text{flat screen TV's}}$


You can use apostrophes to shorten dates, but not to pluralize them. 

Correct | Incorrect 
- | - 
${\text{1980s}}$ | $\redD{\text{1980's}}$
${\text{'80s}}$ | $\redD{\text{80's}}$

*However,* the one time you *do* use an apostrophe to form a plural is when you are pluralizing a single lowercase letter.

For example:

>*There are four p's in the word Mississipi.*
The correct plural form is **dinosaurs**.

items.question_content:
**Choose the option that correctly pluralizes the missing word in this sentence.** 

>Please put all the \_\_\_\_\_\_ in the fridge as soon as you get home.

hints:

Correct | Incorrect 
- | -

You can use apostrophes to shorten dates, but not to pluralize them. 

Correct | Incorrect 
- | - 
${\text{1980s}}$ | $\redD{\text{1980's}}$
${\text{'80s}}$ | $\redD{\text{80's}}$

*However,* the one time you *do* use an apostrophe to form a plural is when you are pluralizing a single lowercase letter.

For example:

>*There are four p's in the word Mississipi.*
The correct plural form is **groceries**.

${\text{1980s}}$ | $\redD{\text{1980's}}$
${\text{'80s}}$ | $\redD{\text{80's}}$

*However,* the one time you *do* use an apostrophe to form a plural is when you are pluralizing a single lowercase letter.

For example:

>*There are four p's in the word Mississipi.*
The correct plural form is **Tuesdays**.

khan_exercises.title: Introduction to the possessive
khan_exercises.description: Learn to use apostrophes to indicate possession, or ownership -- and when not to use them.
topic_path: Punctuation: end-of-sentence punctuation, the comma, and the apostrophe

items.question_content:
**Pick the answer that correctly uses the possessive case in this sentence.**

That piece of pie is  \_\_\_\_\_\_.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The **possessive case** allows us to show that one thing *belongs* to another. To form the possessive case, we simply take a noun and add an apostrophe + the letter `s`:

Noun | Possessive | Example
- | - | -
Benjamin | Benjamin's | Benjamin's guitar
week | week's | A week's time

**However,** when we're making a *pronoun* possessive, *we don't use an apostrophe:*

Pronoun | Possessive | Example
- | - | -
I/me | my/mine | my sister
he/him | his | his house
she/her | her/hers | the hat is hers
it | its | its entrance
they | their/theirs | their favorite restaurant
who | whose | whose car is this?
The correct possessive form is **hers**.

items.question_content:
**Pick the answer that correctly uses the possessive case in this sentence.**

 \_\_\_\_\_\_ loud singing in the car was starting to annoy us.



[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
The **possessive case** allows us to show that one thing *belongs* to another. To form the possessive case, we simply take a noun and add an apostrophe + the letter `s`:

Noun | Possessive | Example
- | - | -
Benjamin | Benjamin's | Benjamin's guitar
week | week's | A week's time

**However,** when we're making a *pronoun* possessive, *we don't use an apostrophe:*

Pronoun | Possessive | Example
- | - | -
I/me | my/mine | my sister
he/him | his | his house
she/her | her/hers | the hat is hers
it | its | its entrance
they | their/theirs | their favorite restaurant
who | whose | whose car is this?
The correct possessive form is **Chris's**.

khan_exercises.title: Advanced (plural) possession
khan_exercises.description: Possessive plurals have a complicated relationship with apostrophes, but this exercise is here to help you understand it!
topic_path: Punctuation: end-of-sentence punctuation, the comma, and the apostrophe

items.question_content:
**Choose the option that correctly uses the apostrophe to show a plural possessive.**

The two \_\_\_\_\_\_ teams are rivals.


[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
To form the **possessive case** for a plural noun ending in `-s`, we simply place an apostrophe *after* the `s`:

Plural noun | Possessive case | Example
- | - | -
wolves | wolves' | the wolves' prey
Joneses | Joneses' | the Joneses' house
The correct possessive form is **schools'**.

items.question_content:
**Choose the option that correctly uses the apostrophe to show a plural possessive.**

I can’t find any clothes I like in the \_\_\_\_\_\_ section.


[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
To form the **possessive case** for a plural noun ending in `-s`, we simply place an apostrophe *after* the `s`:

Plural noun | Possessive case | Example
- | - | -
wolves | wolves' | the wolves' prey
Joneses | Joneses' | the Joneses' house

Since the plural noun "women" doesn't end in `-s`, we can form a normal possessive. 

The correct possessive form is **women's**.
